philosophy: Use as little AI as possible — combine deterministic logic with LLMs.
Ship small, iterate fast. Proof of concept before polish.
Every layer should work independently before integrating with the next.
Tie pricing and value to measurable outcomes, not feature counts.
If you turn the system off and nobody calls within an hour, it's not solving a real problem.
Code is a commodity — protect the system's structural integrity, not the craft of writing it.
Manual coding is the primary source of entropy — AI-generated code with architectural governance is more stable than hand-written code without it.
Natural language is the highest abstraction layer of programming — the quality of output is proportional to the structural precision of input.
Treat AI-generated code as technical debt until simplified — AI favors bloat because it fulfills probabilistic patterns.
Build from first principles — avoid unverified frameworks and black-box agent harnesses. Build a foundation you understand, own, and can debug.

engineering_methodology.description: The engineer's role has shifted from implementation laborer to system governor. Success is measured by architectural intent precision and governance rigor, not syntax proficiency.
core_pillars: 1. component-thinking: govern at the building-block level, not the line level — define interconnections, data flows, and failure modes
2. full-stack-oversight: direct the entire stack to eliminate human bottlenecks — if a project stalls because a specialist is unavailable, the system velocity is wasted
3. communication-clarity: treat natural language as high-precision programming syntax — vague instructions yield broken code
4. ruthless-simplification: strip AI-generated bloat to first principles — a codebase that is simple is a codebase that is understood
5. systematic-testing: maintain ultimate authority over reliability by rejecting AI optimism — the LLM will write passing tests that fail in production

component_design.description: Think in building blocks, not lines of code. Govern the wiring between components.
critical_areas: data-flow-orchestration: design precise pathways for how data moves between backend, storage, and UI
component-wiring: define how modular blocks interact — interfaces between services must be robust and typed
failure-mode-analysis: anticipate cascading failures — architect graceful degradation, not collapse
component_design.constraint_based_research: Before generating code, ground AI agents in external truth — feed specific documentation, architectural references, and established patterns into context. This constrains creative slop and forces adherence to proven tradeoffs.

communication_precision.description: The gap between 'what you want' and 'what you get' is entirely a function of communication quality.
quality_checklist: contextual-grounding: provide the WHY behind this component to align AI logic with your intent
architectural-intent: define data schema, specific libraries, and failure recovery paths
constraint-definition: explicitly forbid known anti-patterns and unnecessary dependencies
ground-truth: supply relevant documentation to constrain generation to current best practices
communication_precision.principle: Move from 'What' to 'Why' — vague instructions yield fake code, precise intent yields production code.

simplification_loop.description: AI models favor bloat because it fulfills probabilistic patterns. Post-generation simplification is mandatory.
steps: 2. build-implement: use AI to generate foundational components
3. test-validate: confirm implementation meets the functional requirement
4. question-research: force AI to justify naming conventions, file structures, every decision made during build
5. ruthless-simplify: remove every line and component that does not break the test suite
simplification_loop.first_principles_audit: Instruct the AI to refactor for absolute minimality — question every abstraction until reaching the point where removing one more line causes failure.

parallel_agency.description: Multiple AI agents build different stack layers simultaneously. The human governor maintains the full picture.
parallel_agency.requirement: A change in the database schema must be instantly reflected in the API contract and frontend state management.
parallel_agency.anti_pattern: Hyper-specialization creates bottlenecks — the governor must be comfortable directing across the entire lifecycle, from local dev to CI/CD to deployment.

patterns: service-layer: business logic lives in services, not routes
repository-pattern: database access abstracted behind repository interfaces
dependency-injection: FastAPI Depends() for composable dependencies
separation-of-concerns: routes handle HTTP, services handle logic, models handle data
error-hierarchy: custom exception classes with typed error responses
async-by-default: async endpoints, sync only when forced by a blocking library
principles: Routes are thin — validate input, call service, return response.
Never import provider SDKs outside the service layer.
Background tasks go through Celery, not FastAPI BackgroundTasks (except fire-and-forget logging).
Every endpoint has input validation (Pydantic), output schema, and error responses documented.
Migrations are always reversible — every upgrade has a matching downgrade.
Environment variables for all configuration — never hardcode secrets, URLs, or feature flags.

validation_framework.role: runtime enforcement — not advisory type hints, but active validation that raises errors immediately on invalid data
validation_framework.principle: Treat every external input as a potential failure point. Shift validation to the absolute source of data entry.

core_patterns.basemodel_over_dataclass: Replace all @dataclass with BaseModel for external data — dataclass type hints are advisory (silent failures), BaseModel enforces at runtime
core_patterns.strict_mode: Use ConfigDict(strict=True) for high-integrity systems where type coercion ambiguity is a risk
core_patterns.field_governance: Use Field() for business rule enforcement — gt/le for numeric bounds, min_length/max_length for strings, pattern for regex validation
core_patterns.literal_types: Use Literal['draft', 'pending', 'shipped'] for categorical fields — eliminates magic string bugs across the application
core_patterns.nested_validation: Pydantic validates the entire tree — parent models containing child models cascade validation down through all levels
core_patterns.reverse_validation: Use model_validate() or ** unpacking to safely ingest raw data from databases and external APIs

lifecycle: 1. instantiation: User(id=1, ...) triggers validation engine immediately
2. validation: raises ValidationError if types or constraints fail
3. transformation: model_dump() exports to standard Python dict
4. serialization: model_dump_json() converts to API-ready JSON string

settings_management.pattern: Inherit from BaseSettings to auto-load environment variables at boot time
settings_management.secret_handling: Use SecretStr for sensitive data — values masked with asterisks in logs
settings_management.boot_time_validation: Instantiate settings at application boot — missing config fails immediately, preventing partial system starts

ai_structured_outputs.purpose: Pydantic is the hard stop for LLM hallucinations — forces AI responses into deterministic structure
patterns: response-format: pass Pydantic model into LLM response_format to force validated JSON output
field-descriptions-as-prompts: Field(description='...') serves as prompt metadata — guides LLM understanding during JSON generation, making code the single source of truth
self-correction-loop: feed ValidationError messages back to LLM programmatically — agent retries until producing valid schema
ai_structured_outputs.principle: Code is the source of truth for both Python logic and the AI's worldview — Field descriptions guide both validation and LLM generation.

day_one_checklist: map every external input (API, DB, webhook) to a BaseModel
replace all @dataclass structures handling external data with BaseModel
implement BaseSettings for all infrastructure credentials with boot-time validation
apply Literal types for all categorical variables — eliminate magic strings
add Field constraints (gt, le, min_length, pattern) for all business-rule-bound fields

key_libraries: fastapi: API framework with automatic OpenAPI docs
pydantic: runtime data validation, schema enforcement, and settings management
sqlalchemy: ORM with async support
alembic: database migration management
celery: distributed task queue for background processing
redis: message broker for Celery + caching layer
uvicorn: ASGI server
httpx: async HTTP client for external API calls
tenacity: retry logic for external service calls
structlog: structured logging
pydantic-settings: environment variable loading with boot-time validation and SecretStr masking
email-validator: EmailStr and domain validation for Pydantic models

patterns: single-database: PostgreSQL handles relational data AND vectors (pgvector) — no separate vector DB needed to start
connection-pooling: use connection pool for all database access
migration-driven: all schema changes go through Alembic, never manual SQL
soft-delete: prefer soft deletes (is_deleted flag) over hard deletes for audit trail
indexed-queries: every query that filters or joins should have a supporting index
principles: PostgreSQL is enough — Instagram scaled to hundreds of millions on just Postgres.
Don't add a separate vector database until pgvector becomes a proven bottleneck.
Redis is for caching and message brokering, not as a primary data store.
Every table has created_at, updated_at timestamps.
Foreign keys are enforced at the database level, not just the application level.
Use UUIDs for public-facing IDs, auto-increment integers for internal references.
scaling_path: Start: single PostgreSQL instance with pgvector
Growth: add read replicas, connection pooling (PgBouncer)
Scale: partition large tables, consider dedicated vector DB (Weaviate/Chroma) only when pgvector query time exceeds SLA

patterns: component-composition: small, reusable components over monolithic pages
shadcn-imports: components are imported into the project (not node_modules) so AI agents can modify them
colocation: keep component, styles, types, and tests together
optimistic-ui: update UI immediately, reconcile with server response
loading-states: every async operation has loading, success, and error states
principles: shadcn/ui for all standard components — don't reinvent buttons, modals, tables, forms.
TypeScript strict mode — no any types except at external API boundaries.
Tailwind for styling — no CSS modules, no styled-components, no inline styles.
Every page works on mobile — responsive by default using Tailwind breakpoints.
API calls go through a centralized client with auth headers, error interceptors, and retry logic.
No direct DOM manipulation — everything through React state and refs.
key_libraries: react: UI framework
vite: dev server and build tool
tailwindcss: utility-first CSS
shadcn-ui: importable component library (not a dependency — lives in your codebase)
zustand: lightweight state management
axios: HTTP client with interceptors
react-router: client-side routing
react-hook-form: performant form handling
zod: schema validation (shared patterns with Pydantic on backend)
lucide-react: icon library (pairs with shadcn)

patterns: minimal-ai: use deterministic logic first, LLMs only when reasoning or generation is required
structured-outputs: always request structured (JSON) outputs from LLMs, validate with Pydantic
chain-of-responsibility: break complex tasks into sequential steps, each independently testable
hybrid-rag-pipeline: ingest → chunk → embed → index (sparse + dense) → parallel retrieve → RRF fuse → cross-encoder rerank → generate → validate
cognitive-architecture: design data flow diagrams before writing AI code
agentic-retrieval: for complex queries (3+ files), use iterative search → read → evaluate → repeat loop instead of single-pass retrieval
modular-primitives: expose discrete list/search/read tools to agent rather than monolithic retrieval blocks
guardrails: prompt injection detection, PII filtering, output validation on every LLM call
fallback-chains: if primary model fails, fall back to secondary, then to deterministic response
structured-citations: every AI-generated answer includes file name, quote, and line number for verifiable traceability
principles: The AI layer is a thin wrapper — most of the system is regular software engineering.
Every LLM call is traced (input, output, latency, token cost) via Langfuse.
Never expose raw LLM output to users without validation and sanitization.
Embeddings are generated once at ingestion time, not at query time (except for the query itself).
Hybrid retrieval is mandatory — BM25 for keyword precision + dense vectors for semantic recall, fused with RRF, refined with cross-encoder reranking.
Challenge the Vector Database Myth: for corpora under 1M documents, disk-based numpy arrays + BM25S outperform dedicated vector databases in latency and simplicity.
LLM-as-a-judge for automated evaluation, but always validate against human-annotated datasets.
Provider SDKs are isolated behind a service interface — switching providers should be a config change.
Context windows are finite — chunk documents intelligently, don't stuff everything in.
Start with semantic hybrid RAG — upgrade to agentic RAG only when query complexity demands multi-step reasoning.
Agent tools return errors as messages, never raise exceptions — the LLM interprets and course-corrects autonomously.
Enforce hard limits on agent tool calls and file read sizes to prevent context-window DoS.

rag.pipeline: ingest → chunk → embed → index → hybrid-retrieve → fuse → rerank → generate → validate

chunking.hard_limit: 1000 tokens per chunk — enforced, not advisory. Preserves semantic locality and model compatibility.
principles: Chunk boundaries should respect semantic units (paragraphs, sections) not just token counts.
Every chunk carries enough metadata to trace back to its source document and location.
Chunking strategy is an experimental variable — changes must be validated against NDCG@10.

retrieval.description: Vector-only retrieval is insufficient for production. The system dispatches queries concurrently to sparse (BM25) and dense (vector) retrievers, fuses results with RRF, then reranks with a cross-encoder.

sparse_layer.purpose: Keyword precision — captures exact terms, identifiers, alphanumeric codes, and rare vocabulary that dense models ignore.
process: tokenization: deterministic conversion to token IDs with English stop-word filtering
indexing: vocabulary lookup table with TF-style weighting (term frequency relative to corpus)
sparse_layer.limitation: Cannot handle paraphrasing — serves as high-precision anchor, not standalone retriever.

dense_layer.purpose: Semantic mapping — captures meaning and handles paraphrasing by mapping text to high-dimensional vector space.

optimization.normalization: L2 normalization — rescale vectors to unit Euclidean distance
optimization.similarity: dot-product via numpy matrix multiplication (not cosine_similarity — L2 normalization makes them equivalent, dot product is faster)
optimization.rationale: Pure matrix algebra provides near-instantaneous retrieval across the corpus without dedicated vector database overhead.

persistence.loading: indices loaded directly into memory from disk

fusion.purpose: Scale-agnostic merging of BM25 scores and dense similarity scores (which exist on different numerical scales).
process: 1. concurrent-retrieval: retrieve top-K candidates (K=50) from both BM25 and dense index
2. list-aggregation: collate results into list-of-lists, maintaining original rank per document
3. fused-ranking: apply RRF formula to each document ID in the aggregated lists
4. final-sort: sort by fused scores to produce hybrid ranking
fusion.rationale: The smoothing factor k=60 prevents a single high-ranking result from one retriever from dominating the output, ensuring balanced contribution from both layers.

reranking.purpose: Joint query-document analysis — unlike bi-encoders (which embed query and document separately), cross-encoders analyze them together to capture subtle contextual relevance.
reranking.effect: Contextually mismatched but statistically relevant documents are downgraded; highly relevant documents are promoted to top positions.

additional_techniques: contextual-retrieval: add document context to chunks before embedding for better recall
query-expansion: rephrase user query into multiple variants for broader recall
self-query: extract metadata filters from natural language queries before retrieval

index_persistence.strategy: cached-indexing to minimize latency and recurring API costs
principles: Indices are stored locally and can be version-controlled.
Indices are loaded into memory from disk — no database server required for corpora under 1M documents.
Challenge the Vector Database Myth: for corpora under 1M documents, disk-based numpy arrays outperform dedicated vector databases in latency and operational simplicity.
Transition to managed vector DB (pgvector, Weaviate) only when corpus exceeds 1M documents or multi-tenant isolation is required.

evaluation.description: Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain at Rank 10 — measures retrieval quality based on position of relevant documents in top 10 results.
evaluation.benchmark_reference: BEIR (Benchmarking IR) framework with domain-specific datasets

expected_performance.dense_only: improved semantic recall but misses keyword-dependent identifiers
expected_performance.hybrid_plus_reranking: near 2x improvement over BM25-only (e.g., NDCG@10 ~47 on Financial QA)

metrics: NDCG@10: primary — position-weighted relevance in top 10
retrieval-precision: are the retrieved chunks relevant?
retrieval-recall: did we miss relevant chunks?
answer-faithfulness: is the generated answer grounded in retrieved context?
answer-relevance: does the generated answer address the user's question?

cold_start.description: When proprietary data lacks existing benchmarks, generate synthetic evaluation sets.
cold_start.method: Use high-reasoning LLM to generate BEIR-style (Query, Relationship, Corpus) pairs from document chunks.
cold_start.principle: Never ship a RAG pipeline without an evaluation dataset — synthetic is better than none.

experimental_loop: 1. establish baseline NDCG@10 with current pipeline
2. change one variable (chunking strategy, embedding model, retrieval parameters)
4. keep changes that improve NDCG@10, revert those that don't
5. repeat — every pipeline change is validated scientifically, not by vibes

retrieval_paradigms.description: Two paradigms exist — choose based on query complexity, latency budget, and accuracy requirements.

semantic_rag.correction_capability: none — static output from single retrieval
semantic_rag.best_for: straightforward queries, high-volume low-latency, FAQ-style lookups
semantic_rag.limitation: fails when answer requires synthesizing from multiple sources or when initial retrieval misses the target

agentic_rag.correction_capability: high — autonomous recovery via feedback loop
agentic_rag.best_for: complex queries spanning 3+ files, engineering wikis, codebase navigation, multi-step reasoning
agentic_rag.advantage: mimics human engineer persistence — if first search is irrelevant, agent recognizes failure and pivots

toolkit.description: Modular primitives exposed to the LLM for autonomous file system navigation.
primitives: list: pattern matching (glob) to identify relevant files — use relative paths for token optimization
search (grab): compiled regex for case-insensitive search across document lines — returns file names and line numbers
read: content extraction with path validation (is_relative_to) to prevent directory traversal attacks
toolkit.discovery_pattern: list the big picture → search for the needle → read the context to verify
toolkit.high_performance_search: ripgrep via subprocess — Rust-based, respects .gitignore, ignores hidden directories, gold standard for codebase RAG

context_window_dos_prevention: agent_request_limit: hard cap on tool calls per query to prevent infinite loops
read_max_lines: limit file ingestion size to prevent context exhaustion from large files
timeout: maximum wall-clock time per agent execution
production_safety.error_handling: return human-readable error messages, never raise exceptions in agent tools — treat the LLM as a system administrator that interprets errors and course-corrects

structured_citations.requirement: every answer must include verifiable citations
structured_citations.purpose: enables downstream validation by human auditors and automated verification systems
structured_citations.implementation: Pydantic models enforce citation structure in agent outputs

decision_framework: 1. does the answer require synthesizing from 3+ files? → agentic RAG
2. is target response time milliseconds? → semantic RAG. Can user wait 10-15 seconds? → agentic RAG
3. does deployment support subprocess execution (ripgrep)? If restricted serverless → semantic RAG
4. is there budget for multiple LLM calls per query? If constrained → semantic RAG with hybrid retrieval
5. default: start with semantic hybrid RAG, upgrade to agentic when query complexity demands it

context_architecture.description: Context management is the single biggest lever for AI quality. Minimize token bloat while maximizing relevance.

tiered_loading.description: Three-tier hierarchy to minimize token consumption while preserving navigation ability.
levels: level-0 (abstract): one-line summary per area — agent scans entire system architecture in under 2,000 tokens
level-1 (overview): short description of purpose, workflows, and inter-area relationships
level-2 (full): deep-level data accessed only when agent confirms relevance from level 0-1
tiered_loading.principle: Load the minimum context needed for the agent to decide if it needs more. Never front-load full documents.

context_hub_structure: identity: mission, values, system personality — loaded into every system prompt
inbox: temporary staging for unprocessed ideas and tasks
areas: long-term responsibilities (e.g., clients, products, operations)
knowledge: permanent reference library — SOPs, documentation, domain guides
archive: stale data — agents are explicitly instructed to ignore this to prevent context clutter
context_architecture.storage: Git-versioned Markdown files — version-controlled, diffable, editable locally and synced to cloud
principles: Context is King — the quality of AI output is determined by the quality of context, not the model.
Separate structured data (PostgreSQL) from unstructured context (Markdown/Git).
Agent skills and instructions are defined in version-controlled Markdown files — installable, removable, auditable.
Every agent interaction starts with identity context (who am I, what are my values) before task context.

key_libraries: langchain: LLM orchestration, chains, document loaders, text splitters
langgraph: stateful agent workflows with cycles and branching
langfuse: LLM observability, tracing, prompt management
pydantic: structured output validation
tiktoken: token counting for context window management
sentence-transformers: local embedding models (fallback / offline)
bm25s: sparse retrieval with BM25 scoring (Lucene method)
numpy: high-performance vector operations, index persistence (.npy)
cohere: cross-encoder reranking API
pydantic-ai: tool-call orchestration with strict data validation for agent outputs
ripgrep: Rust-based high-performance search for agentic RAG (subprocess, respects .gitignore)
sentry-sdk: error and exception tracking

patterns: container-per-service: backend, frontend, worker, redis, postgres each in their own container
docker-compose-environments: separate compose files for dev, test, and production
health-checks: every service exposes a /health endpoint
twelve-factor: config in environment, logs to stdout, stateless processes
blue-green-deployment: zero-downtime deploys by running new version alongside old
load-balancing: Nginx as reverse proxy with upstream blocks for horizontal scaling
principles: Docker Compose for development and single-server production — Kubernetes only at true scale.
Every service has a health check that verifies its dependencies (DB connection, Redis ping).
Secrets are never in Docker images or git — use environment variables injected at runtime.
HTTPS everywhere — Certbot/Let's Encrypt for automated certificate management.
Logs are structured JSON to stdout — centralized logging is a layer on top, not baked in.
CI/CD runs tests, builds images, and deploys on merge to main — no manual deploys.
Monitor cost, latency, and error rates post-deployment — not just 'is it up?'

patterns: cache-aside: check cache first, fall back to DB, populate cache on miss
ttl-based: all cache entries have explicit TTL, no indefinite caching
cache-invalidation: invalidate on write, not on timer

load_balancing.scaling_path: vertical first (bigger VPS), horizontal when CPU consistently >70%

patterns: idempotent-tasks: every task can be safely retried
dead-letter-queue: failed tasks after N retries go to DLQ for manual inspection
priority-queues: separate queues for urgent (auth, notifications) vs. bulk (ingestion, reports)
task-timeout: every task has a max execution time

event_driven.description: Redis + Celery create a logical disconnect between API requests and processing — enables unattended 24/7 execution, absorbs traffic bursts without overwhelming the server.
use_cases: user-created: triggers welcome email and default settings
webhook-received: raw payload persisted to DB, then async dispatched to worker

steps: 1. signature-verification: verify incoming webhook against provider signatures (e.g., Stripe, WhatsApp/Meta) to prevent unauthorized injection
2. data-persistence: immediately save raw payload to PostgreSQL — leaves a trace for debugging even if downstream processing fails
3. async-dispatch: hand off to Celery worker, freeing the API to handle the next event instantly
webhook_handling.principle: Never process webhook payloads synchronously — always persist first, process async.

operational_tiers.description: Three-tiered model separating automated triggers, recurring schedules, and conversational agents. Start with Tiers 1-2 for reliability, add Tier 3 for dynamic intelligence.

tier_1_triggers.name: Trigger-Based Actions
tier_1_triggers.reliability: highest — deterministic logic, no LLM involved

tier_2_scheduled.name: Scheduled Workflows
tier_2_scheduled.advanced: DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph) system in Python for complex multi-step workflows — provides n8n/Zapier-style sequential logic within controlled code

tier_3_agents.name: Agent Layer
tier_3_agents.pattern: dynamic, generative decision-making based on user input
tier_3_agents.reliability: experimental — requires human-in-the-loop refinement and guardrails
tier_3_agents.principle: Agents are powerful but volatile. Gate agent actions behind confirmation for anything destructive or irreversible.

scaling_path: Start: single VPS with Docker Compose (backend + frontend + postgres + redis)
Growth: separate database to managed PostgreSQL, add Celery workers
Scale: load balancer (Nginx) + multiple backend instances, CDN for frontend
Enterprise: container orchestration (ECS/Kubernetes), managed Redis, read replicas

patterns: defense-in-depth: multiple layers of security, not a single perimeter
least-privilege: every role gets minimum required permissions
fail-secure: on error, deny access rather than grant it
input-validation: validate all user input at the API boundary (Pydantic)
output-encoding: sanitize all output to prevent XSS
principles: Never store plaintext passwords — bcrypt with sufficient work factor.
JWT access tokens are short-lived (15-30 min), refresh tokens are long-lived and rotated.
CORS is restrictive by default — whitelist specific origins, not '*'.
Rate limit authentication endpoints aggressively (10/min per IP).
All secrets in environment variables, never in code or config files committed to git.
SQL injection is prevented by parameterized queries (SQLAlchemy handles this) — never raw string concatenation.
File uploads are validated (type, size, content) and stored outside the web root.
API keys are hashed in the database, not stored in plaintext.
HTTPS enforced everywhere — no HTTP endpoints in production.
Audit log for all authentication events and permission changes.
webhook_security: Verify every incoming webhook against provider signatures (Stripe, Meta/WhatsApp, GitHub) — never trust unverified payloads.
Persist raw payload to database before processing — leaves audit trail even if downstream logic fails.
Rate limit webhook endpoints separately from user-facing API endpoints.
ai_security: Prompt injection detection on all user inputs that reach LLM calls.
PII filtering on LLM outputs before returning to users.
Output validation — LLM responses are validated against expected schema before use.
Context isolation — user A's documents are never retrievable by user B.
Token budget enforcement — no single request can consume unbounded tokens.
Model output is never trusted as code execution input without sanitization.

track: request-latency: p50, p95, p99 per endpoint
llm-cost: tokens consumed and dollar cost per call and per session
llm-latency: time to first token, total generation time
retrieval-quality: precision and recall per RAG query
task-queue-depth: Celery queue length and processing time
agent-budget: per-agent API cost tracking with hard caps
webhook-throughput: events received, processed, failed per provider

budget_control.description: Without strict controls, a single autonomous agent loop can easily exceed $50 in API costs.
mechanisms: max-turns: hard cap on agent reasoning iterations per session
per-session-budget: dollar limit per agent invocation
model-tiering: use cheaper models for classification/routing, expensive models only for final generation
alert-thresholds: notify when daily/weekly spend exceeds configured percentage of budget

principles: Every LLM call is traced end-to-end: input, output, latency, tokens, cost.
Structured logs (JSON) with correlation IDs across request lifecycle.
Alerts on error rate spikes, not just individual errors.
Health checks verify actual functionality (can query DB, can reach Redis), not just process uptime.
Persist a trace of every event in the database — logs and DB traces are the only way to debug an evolving AI system.
Monitor cost as aggressively as you monitor errors — an uncontrolled agent loop is a financial DoS.

patterns: unit-tests: service layer logic, utility functions, validators
integration-tests: API endpoints with real database (not mocks)
llm-tests: deterministic inputs with expected output patterns, LLM-as-judge for quality
rag-evaluation: retrieval precision/recall on annotated dataset
e2e-tests: critical user flows through the full stack
principles: Integration tests hit a real database — mocks hide real bugs.
LLM outputs are tested for structure and constraints, not exact text.
Every bug fix comes with a regression test.
Tests run in Docker to match production environment.
CI blocks merge on test failure — no exceptions.

ai_optimism_defense.description: The most dangerous failure in AI-era engineering is AI Optimism — LLMs write tests that pass by mocking every difficult dependency, creating green lights that fail in production.
mandates: reject-mocking-trap: actively reject tests that mock away the logic being validated — if the AI mocks the database response instead of testing integration, the test is a liability
edge-case-authority: the human identifies outliers AI misses — network latency, race conditions, malformed inputs, empty states, concurrent writes
framework-mastery: you must understand pytest/vitest at a level where you can audit AI-generated test logic, not just run it
automated-governance: no code enters main without systematic automated validation — testing is in CI/CD, not optional
ai_optimism_defense.question: Ask 'What could really go wrong here?' — AI handles the happy path, the human architect identifies the outliers.

product_strategy.description: Discovery and validation methodology — determines what to build before any code is written. Applies to founder and PM hats.

discovery.principle: Discovery is a filter, not a sales pitch. If clear ROI cannot be established, the project is a non-starter.
protocol: uncover-actual-problem: look past initial requests — the 'big pressing problem' often obscures simpler, high-impact opportunities
brutal-roi-honesty: do not proceed without clear, measurable return on investment
shadow-real-users: spend time watching actual employees work — hidden processes automatable with simple logic often offer the highest impact
quick-wins-first: select use cases that yield immediate results to build trust before tackling complex overhauls
red_flags: ai-for-ai-sake: no clear business problem → leads to pilot purgatory with no production path
no-success-criteria: without KPIs, the project will be perceived as failure regardless of technical quality
magic-expectations: expecting 100% accuracy on day one → will churn during inevitable iteration phase
restricted-data-access: security roadblocks stall development and kill momentum
discovery.breakage_definition: Look for processes that are painful, repetitive, costly, AND unfixed. If all four boxes are checked, there is a serious opportunity.

proven_use_cases: document-processing: automating extraction and validation against company standards
content-generation: structured, template-based creation for marketing or reporting
customer-support-agents: handling 80% of repetitive tickets with clear escalation paths for the 20%
internal-knowledge-assistants: RAG-based systems for proprietary documentation
data-extraction: transforming unstructured data into structured formats for downstream systems
use_case_selection_checklist: repeatability: is the task structured enough for automation?
human-escalation: is there a defined path for edge cases the AI cannot handle?
error-impact: what is the cost of a hallucination? (must be low for customer-facing, high-oversight for critical)
structured-rules: can success be defined by a set of logical rules?

accuracy_paradigm.v1_accuracy: 70-80% — frame as iterative development, not finished product
accuracy_paradigm.v2_accuracy: 90% — requires rigorous iteration with domain expert feedback
accuracy_paradigm.principle: Shift mindset from 'perfection' to 'iteration' — clients must be hands-on domain experts, not outsource-and-forget.

proof_of_concept.confidence: used when technical path is unproven
proof_of_concept.deliverable: local demo, script, or technical report

minimum_viable_product.confidence: used when solution is repeatable and proven

mvp_vs_poc.rule: Never blur the lines between a demo and a product.

proposal_framework: problem-statement: rephrase the friction in the user's own words
scope-boundaries: explicitly list what the system will NOT do
technical-approach: architecture diagrams showing how the stack solves the problem
success-measurement: specific KPIs for the first iteration
ongoing-costs: estimates for API usage, infrastructure, and maintenance
product_strategy.turn_it_off_test: If you turn the system off and nobody notices within an hour, it's not solving a real problem. The faster they call, the better your product.

delivery.description: High-velocity delivery methodology — how to ship fast, scope correctly, and maintain quality.

sprint_model.rationale: Deliver tangible value before internal bureaucracy has time to stall the project.
anatomy: day-1: kickoff call — align on communication channels and finalize KPIs
day-2: scaffolding — fork the standard project template, establish repo structure
days-3-8: core development — build workflows, LLM integration, quality iteration
days-9-12: internal testing — edge cases, error handling, UI polish
days-13-14: final demo and ship to production

scoping_discipline.principle: Fixed-boundary proposals prevent scope creep. Every proposal explicitly lists what the system will NOT do.
patterns: outcome-based-pricing: charge per sprint or outcome, not hourly — shifts value from time spent to results delivered
value-based-pricing: tie price to measurable business impact (tickets resolved, hours saved, revenue generated)
recurring-sprints: software is never done — sell recurring sprints for new capabilities and maintenance

communication.principle: Protect deep work — save meetings for kickoff and demo only.
patterns: async-updates: progress via Slack/Markdown reports, not daily standups
meeting-discipline: only kickoff call and final demo — no status meetings
error-resolution: Sentry → extract trace as Markdown → feed to AI → generate and review fix

rapid_fix_loop.description: Fix production bugs in minutes, not hours.
steps: 1. detect: Sentry alerts via Slack with production error
2. extract: copy error trace as Markdown
3. prompt: feed trace + relevant context into AI
4. execute: generate, review, and deploy fix immediately

pipeline_management.principle: After sprint 1, clients often disappear for 2-8 weeks to test internally. This is a cash flow killer.
mitigation: strategic-over-planning: assume 50% latency rate between sprints, fill schedule accordingly
pipeline-buffering: engineers move to internal tool development or waiting-list projects when clients go quiet

standardization.principle: Same stack, same patterns, same file structure across every project. If you know where everything is in one project, you know where it is in all of them.
benefits: a small team scales output 10x without proportional headcount increase
new projects start from a fork of the standard template — not from scratch
AI agents are more effective when codebase structure is predictable and consistent

scaling_philosophy.principle: Scale through software leverage, not headcount. Recruiting and training overhead causes profits to plummet.
scaling_philosophy.golden_era: The market hasn't caught up to AI-native efficiency yet — use this gap to maximize output with minimal team size.
recurring_revenue: recurring feature sprints for new capabilities
scaling_philosophy.long_game: Models change every month, but processes must remain stable. Swap underlying LLMs as they improve without reinventing the delivery model.

ai_development_workflow.description: Structured AI-assisted development discipline. Reject vibe coding — treat AI as a tool with a human governor.

context_management.principle: Every AI session starts fresh. Never let the AI re-index the whole codebase.
patterns: context-priming: point AI to 2-3 specific files and relevant documentation — prevents hallucinations and saves context window
plan-then-execute: verify AI's logic in plan mode before allowing code generation
skills-folder: maintain reusable AI skill definitions as Markdown files — repeatable tasks become standardized prompts

governor_checklist: naming-conventions: does AI-generated code match the project's established patterns?
folder-structure: are files in the correct directories per the standard template?
architectural-integrity: has the AI introduced non-standard libraries or clever logic that breaks the pattern?
dependency-audit: has the AI added unnecessary dependencies that increase the attack surface or maintenance burden?
ai_development_workflow.principle: The developer does not just check if code works — they ensure the AI hasn't deviated from standards. You are the governor, not the typist.

claude: all sections — reads stack profile to validate agent recommendations and govern execution
